assertion_results.text: Data extraction identifies the ecosystem as RPM based on the library name (openssl-libs) and component context, not Cargo (Step 1 Ecosystem detection — read ecosystems from Ecosystem Mappings config)
assertion_results.evidence: In data-extraction.md, the Ecosystem Detection section explicitly states: 'The vulnerable library is openssl-libs, which is an RPM system package (not a Cargo crate, npm package, or Go module).' It confirms the ecosystem as RPM based on three factors: (1) the library name openssl-libs being a well-known RPM package, (2) the version format following RPM NEVRA conventions, and (3) the security-matrix.md Ecosystem Mappings table listing RPM with rpms.lock.yaml. The final determination is 'Ecosystem: RPM'.

assertion_results.text: Version impact table includes ALL versions from the 2.2.x stream supportability matrix (2.2.0 through 2.2.4) and uses rpms.lock.yaml data for version extraction (Step 2.3)
assertion_results.evidence: In version-impact.md, the Version Impact Table lists all 5 versions: 2.2.0 (3.0.7-25.el9_3, YES), 2.2.1 (3.0.7-27.el9_4, YES), 2.2.2 (3.0.7-27.el9_4, YES), 2.2.3 (3.0.7-28.el9_4, NO), and 2.2.4 (3.0.7-28.el9_4, NO). The Dependency Version Extraction section explicitly references rpms.lock.yaml as the data source with commands like 'git show v0.4.5:rpms.lock.yaml | grep openssl-libs'.

assertion_results.text: Dependency chain context classifies the package origin as explicit install because openssl-libs is present in rpms.lock.yaml, not inherited from the base image (Step 2.3.5)
assertion_results.evidence: In version-impact.md, the Dependency Chain Context (Step 2.3.5) section states: 'openssl-libs is present in rpms.lock.yaml at all checked tags (v0.4.5, v0.4.8, v0.4.11, v0.4.12). Per the classification rules: In lock file --> explicit install. The package is explicitly installed via the RPM lock file, not inherited from the base image.' The classification is clearly 'explicit install' based on presence in rpms.lock.yaml.

assertion_results.text: Remediation creates a single task for the Konflux release repo — not the two-task upstream backport + downstream propagation flow used for source dependency ecosystems (Step 7 Remediation Task Creation — system package path)
assertion_results.evidence: In remediation.md, the section 'Ecosystem: RPM -- Single Task' explicitly states: 'Because openssl-libs is an RPM system package (not a source dependency like Cargo/npm/Go), the remediation follows the single task pattern. The fix happens directly in the Konflux release repo. There is no upstream backport + downstream propagation split.' The closing note also reaffirms: 'Only one task is created because this is an RPM ecosystem vulnerability. Source dependency ecosystems (Cargo, npm, Go) would produce two tasks (upstream backport + downstream propagation), but RPM packages are fixed directly in the Konflux release repo.' Only a single Jira create_issue call is shown.

assertion_results.text: Remediation task description follows task-description-template.md format with Repository, Target Branch, Description, Implementation Notes, and Acceptance Criteria sections — Files to Modify is intentionally omitted per remediation-templates.md (Step 7 Remediation Task Creation)
assertion_results.evidence: In remediation.md, the task description contains the following sections: 'Repository' (rhtpa-release.0.4.z), 'Target Branch' (main), 'Description' (remediation details), 'Implementation Notes' (update steps), and 'Acceptance Criteria' (checklist items). There is no 'Files to Modify' section present in the task description, which is consistent with the assertion that it is intentionally omitted per remediation-templates.md for RPM system packages.
